title: Nested Loops (O(n²))
code: static int[] TwoSumBrute(int[] nums, int target)
for (int j = i + 1; j < nums.Length; j++)

nums.label: nums (every pair (i, j) with j > i is checked)

frames.caption: Brute force: try every pair (i, j) with j > i and test nums[i] + nums[j] == 26. With n = 4 there are n(n-1)/2 = 6 pairs to check at most.
frames.note: This is the O(n²) baseline the hash solution is measured against. No structure is exploited — we just enumerate all pairs. The "comparisons" counter makes the quadratic cost visible: it can reach n(n-1)/2, which grows like n². Keep an eye on how much faster it climbs than linear search's ~n counter.

frames.caption: Outer loop: fix i=0 (nums[0] = 2). The inner loop will pair it with every j > 0.
frames.note: The outer loop picks the first element of the pair. For this i, the inner loop will scan every j to its right — that nested scan is what multiplies the cost. One loop is O(n); a loop INSIDE a loop over the same data is O(n) × O(n) = O(n²). Reading complexity off the loop SHAPE like this (nested same-n loop ⇒ quadratic) is the skill the reviewer drills.

frames.caption: i=0, j=1: nums[0] + nums[1] = 2 + 7 = 9 ≠ 26 — not a match, advance j. (comparisons = 1)
frames.note: Each inner-loop body is one unit of O(n²) work: a single comparison of the pair (nums[i], nums[j]). The outer loop fixes i (n choices) and the inner loop tries every later j, so the total number of pairs is n + (n-1) + ... + 1 = n(n-1)/2 — about half of n², which is still Θ(n²). Watch the comparisons counter: it grows like the square of the input, which is exactly what "quadratic time" means. Contrast linear search, whose counter ticks once per element (~n), and binary search, which halves the range each step (~log n).

frames.caption: i=0, j=2: nums[0] + nums[2] = 2 + 11 = 13 ≠ 26 — not a match, advance j. (comparisons = 2)
frames.note: Each inner-loop body is one unit of O(n²) work: a single comparison of the pair (nums[i], nums[j]). The outer loop fixes i (n choices) and the inner loop tries every later j, so the total number of pairs is n + (n-1) + ... + 1 = n(n-1)/2 — about half of n², which is still Θ(n²). Watch the comparisons counter: it grows like the square of the input, which is exactly what "quadratic time" means. Contrast linear search, whose counter ticks once per element (~n), and binary search, which halves the range each step (~log n).

The outer loop fixes i (n choices) and the inner loop tries every later j, so the total number of pairs is n + (n-1) + ... + 1 = n(n-1)/2 — about half of n², which is still Θ(n²). frames.caption: Outer loop: fix i=2 (nums[2] = 11). The inner loop will pair it with every j > 2.
frames.note: The outer loop picks the first element of the pair. For this i, the inner loop will scan every j to its right — that nested scan is what multiplies the cost. One loop is O(n); a loop INSIDE a loop over the same data is O(n) × O(n) = O(n²). Reading complexity off the loop SHAPE like this (nested same-n loop ⇒ quadratic) is the skill the reviewer drills.

frames.caption: i=2, j=3: nums[2] + nums[3] = 11 + 15 = 26 == 26 — found the pair after 6 comparisons. Return [2, 3].
frames.note: An early return ends the scan, so this lucky input finished in 6 comparison(s) — that is the BEST case. The WORST case (no pair, or the match is the very last pair) checks all n(n-1)/2 = 6 pairs, which is Θ(n²). That worst-case quadratic count is the headline complexity. The O(n) hash version of Two Sum collapses this inner scan into a single O(1) lookup, turning n(n-1)/2 comparisons into ~n.
